author: @CairnOfDunnCroftPermaculture
text:
We're about to rip out the old coal back boiler and build a rocket mass heater in its place. Next year I'll build a rocket cooker/water heater, at which point the house will be self sufficient for heating, hot water and cooking, on small amounts of coppice wood. It's at that point I'll take my house off mains power, because I'll only be using it for refrigeration, lighting, and computers. 
Just about everything we do is about personal resiliency, and never having to worry about the cost of heating is going to be amazing.

If this system could be small enough, it would be great for campers/RVers, as it might be cheaper/lighter/more efficient to produce hydrogen, store it in balloons, and use it for cooking and running AC. (btw, think you could blow up a regular party balloon and one painted with your ink to see how long they'll hold the hydrogen before it escapes?  I'm thinking your ink will help hold the hydrogen)

author: @jpsimon206
text: Years ago I lived in Tucson Arizona in a traditional Pueblo house, about 430 ft². Summer temperatures were often 110° f all day long. These houses were built of mud, typically with 2 ft thick walls. The combination of small rooms and thick walls created a thermal battery. All of the original pueblos had very primitive swamp coolers, typically only two square feet. These would be enough to keep the home perfectly comfortable all year round. There were careful formulas for eavelength and window sizes to maintain this formula. The sun's heat would take all day to penetrate the walls and warm them up. Night times in the desert are very cold so this warmth was very welcome. By morning time the walls have cooled off to atmospheric and they stay cool all day long right up until sundown. There are tons of less complicated and specialized building techniques that can save a small fortune in heating and cooling costs. Simply facing south with a heavily windowed wall makes an enormous difference. It's sad how much of this simple wisdom has been lost to the conveniences of products like AC. How many generations pass before this information is simply lost?

replies.author: @gr1f1th
replies.text: I live in Tucson now. I lived in a sump block house with a reflective roof. And rented a small Casita as well. Though the swamp cooler had been removed on both. Swamp coolers don't work for months during monsoon season (too humid) and use 6 gallons of water per hour when they do work. The water crisis here is about to get very real with the Hover dam about to shut off water to Arizona. The sump block is a thermal battery and it radiated heat indoors ALL day and all night... during most of the summer night time only falls to the high 80's f for a few hours before sunrise. I thought I'd like those houses better but I'm building a house that is 2x the square foot and yet half the power usage for cooling.

replies.author: @jpsimon206
replies.text: @gr1f1th no question there are far better techniques. I live in the northwest now and we use drums full of water as a thermal battery in the greenhouse. When I lived in Tucson, I was a very poor student, I do not remember there being any monsoon season? Perhaps global warming? I thought it was worth pointing out these incredibly simple relatively passive systems in the context of this video. I was not suggesting this was a better way to build

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text:
Quite right. Batteries end up being the most expensive part of a solar setup, so anything you can do to reduce the need to store power is good.
What I'd consider doing if he had space is insulating ~2000l of water. When the sun is out you can cool that water down with a heat pump, and it can then be circulated in radiators using a small 12V pump with air blowing over it.
You can store 87000BTU by cooling 2000l of water from 16C to 5C. I hope my calculations are correct. On hot sunny days you can directly power the air conditioning, but on muggy days you'd rely on stored cold water.

replies.author: @christianeaton

author: @georgemckenzie2525
text:
Throughout the seventies envelope houses were built in northern Vermont, one local example used the old  granite foundation stones as a heat soak in the crawl space beneath,  very efficient ... at putting radon vapor into the home. 
Not all rocks are ideally suited for breathable systems contact. 
 The asbestos mine tailings from Belvidere would make, and have, made excellent roads,  but that pesky fiber  keeps them from being deployed much these days

author: @totherarf
text:
One thing that struck me was how much energy is in air!

We used to stop for the occasional weekend with a group at an old lead mine in Derbyshire. The mine was not capped , but sealed with a grille that you could shine a torch down to get an idea of the depth (well you couldn't because we never got a torch that could get that far). 
One of the tricks was to wait until the temp had dropped at night and go out and look at the plume of water vapor boiling out of the grille. 
Heating was by wood burner so inevitably someone would get a shovel full of red hot coals and dump it down the shaft. .... I kid you not, it would produce the biggest firework as the rising air stopped the coals from falling and then jetted them back out! That is what a temp differential of a few degrees can do with the right plumbing!

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Ever since I started using solar and listening to your channel I have also changed all my lights into 12v LED options. My fridge fund from inverted solar as does my TV, gas heater fan and many other things. I use the grid to boil water for a coffee and to use the washing machine and dish washer but almost everything else is solar while batteries last. This is why I am looking at your sterling engine idea with magnets and coils to reclaim some energy from anything producing heat. Looking back to move forward has some advantages.

author: @Coxeysbodgering
text:
Fingers crossed next year I get a couple of acres and plan living off grid, either in a static caravan or barn/stable conversion. I've broke energy needs down to what can I power on 12v DC?  lighting, laptop (using car cigarette transformer I already have) phone charging and other usb with adapted  car chargers. What can I power with my modified sine wave inverter? 240v lighting, non motor and minimal start up load devices. What do I need that requires 240v sine wave power? What can I do without? What can I do elsewhere? Laurette, family. So do I need to invest in a pure sine wave inverter or do without?
Cooking via gas hottle and heating when necessary, hot water from solar water heating and dump load of wind generation. Etc if you think that way, whether 24/48 or 12v system you are efficiently using the energy with having to unnecessarily step up or down and loose energy in the conversions

author: @alaingrignon
text: Microgeneration (home-based generation) should be thought of as an ecosystem. You want a mix of generating technologies, scavenging technologies, storage, and efficiency tech. There is an infinite number of permutations that can be used with those to get to a mix that is right for you. What is lacking is a single source of knowledge one can go to, to make informed decisions as to which tech to use where, how to implement those technologies, and how to calculate your Total Cost of Ownership for them (the ultimate goal is to save/generate money). Or I could have just said, yes Rob, you're right! :)

replies.author: @porcelaingiant2965

author: @Rayxl1
text: Thank you for an interesting video. I'm glad I took the trouble to make a woodstove out of scrap metal. Most heating and cooking though the winter costs me nothing as I burn mostly old pallets that the companies in the local business park are glad to see the back of.

replies.author: @EgonSorensen
replies.text:
USDA regulations require manufacturers to treat wooden pallets before shipping to prevent the spread of pathogens. They either treat pallets by fumigating them with methyl bromide, a toxic pesticide, or by heat-treating them in kilns. For reusing purposes, look for pallets stamped with an “HT”, which means they haven’t been exposed to chemicals (at least not in the treating process.) 
Avoid unmarked pallets or those with “MB” for methyl bromide - as it is toxic
